content.full_text:
November 26, 1991


Gerry Chalmers
Federal Consistency Staff
California Coastal Commission
45 Fremont, Suite 2000
San Francisco, CA  94105-2219

                                        Re:  Your Request for Informal Assistance
                                             Our File No. I-91-366

Dear Mr. Chalmers:

     This is in response to your letter requesting advice
regarding your responsibilities as a member of the Federal
Consistency Staff of the California Coastal Commission under the
"revolving-door" and new "ethics" provisions of the Political
Reform Act (the "Act").1    Since your need to seek new employment
at this time is uncertain and you have not become involved in
negotiations for future employment, we are treating your letter as
a request for informal assistance.2

                              QUESTIONS

     1.  As an intern with the California Coastal Commission, what
restrictions are imposed on your participation in Coastal
Commission matters relating to persons with whom you may be
negotiating about, or may have arranged, prospective employment?

     2.  At the time you terminate your employment with the
California Coastal Commission, what restrictions will there be on
your post-employment activity?


_______________
1    Government Code Sections 81000-91015.  All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.  Commission
regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations Section
18000, et seq.  All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.

2    Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the
immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice. (Section
83114; Regulation 18329(c)(3).)


428 J Street, Suite 800 ● P.O. Box 807 ● Sacramento CA 95804-0807 ● (916) 322-5660

Our File No. I-91-366
Page 2


CONCLUSIONS

1. As a designated employee of the California Coastal
Commission, you are prohibited from making, participating in
making, or using your official position to influence, any
governmental decision directly relating to any person with whom
you are negotiating, or to have an arrangement concerning,
prospective employment.

2. You may not represent or act as agent or attorney for any
person before the California Coastal Commission for compensation,
for the purpose of influencing administrative action for one year.

In addition, you may not represent any other person before
the California Coastal Commission with respect to any federal
consistency review in which you monitored or participated. This
restriction has no time limit.

FACTS

You have been an intern with the Coastal Commission's Federal
Consistency Program since June, 1990. The Federal Consistency
Program is based on the Federal Coastal Zone Management Act which
gives the California Coastal Commission limited regulatory
authority with respect to federal and federally permitted
activities that affect the coastal zone. Under the Federal
Coastal Zone Management Act, the Coastal Commission may review
specified federal licenses and permits which affect land and
water, or affect the resources of the coastal zone directly or
indirectly through spill-over impacts.

As an intern you are a designated employee required to
complete an annual statement of economic interests. As an intern
you have been involved in the following:

1. General activities such as the production of a newsletter
to local governments describing the proceedings of workshops
conducted by the Federal Consistency Staff.

2. The monitoring of past federal consistency projects to
determine the success of mitigation measures implemented by the
Coastal Commission.

3. In addition you participate in the review of negative
determination statements and environmental impact statements.
When a project involves sites under the Coastal Commission's
jurisdiction, the responsible entity will file a negative
determination statement and a draft environmental impact statement
if they believe that the project will have only a de minimis
impact on the Coastal Commission's jurisdiction. Your duties
include communicating with the applicant and other involved
persons or agencies, drafting letters, preparation of a staff
report (with recommendations), and potentially presenting the

Our File No. I-91-366
Page 3


report to the Coastal Commission. You stated, however, that no
project in which you have been involved has proceeded to the point
of a staff report to the Coastal Commission.

4. Finally, between August and November 1990 you
participated in the preparation of comments on the Environmental
Impact Report/Environmental Impact Statement (EIR/EIS) prepared
for a proposed toll road in Orange County by the San Joaquin Hills
Transportation Corridor (the "project"). The EIR/EIS was
published and circulated by the Transportation Corridor Agency.
You stated that you reviewed and annotated the EIR/EIS for review
by your supervisor. In cooperation with your supervisor, you then
wrote a draft version of Coastal Commission comments on the
project's EIR/EIS, which was reviewed, edited, revised and signed
by your supervisor. In addition, you handled inquiries from other
agencies and the public on the document/project, or procedural
aspects concerning the comments and researched jurisdictional
questions concerning the project should it continue. You stated
that the issuance of Coastal Commission comments often depends on
staff workload and are generally informal, with no substantive
bearing on any subsequent formal proceedings on the project. To
date, no formal application on the project has been initiated at
the Coastal Commission.

You also stated that all the activities described above are
highly supervised. All public documents and letters which you
helped prepare are signed by your supervisor or any policy,
legal, or other matter on which you work is directed by, and
coordinated with, a supervisor. In some cases, legal clearance
with the Coastal Commission's legal staff or coordination with
higher officers is required.

Recently you were notified that due to the California budget
emergency your position may be eliminated. Consequently, you have
started considering potential future employment. Two persons you
have considered contacting are involved in the project EIR/EIS.
One is a private planning consultant who represents various
businesses, governmental agencies and public interest groups. The
planning consultant wrote comments on the project's EIR/EIS
behalf of clients in opposition to the project. The planning
consultant may be involved in the future in proceedings before the
Coastal Commission or litigation involving the Coastal
Commission's decisions concerning the project.

Another potential employer is a law firm representing
plaintiffs who are challenging the adequacy of the EIR for the
project. Again, with regards to the project, this firm has had no
direct formal involvement with the Coastal Commission or its
staff, except through informal comments and inquiries. However,
should formal federal consistency review by the Commission occur,
the firm could be representing clients in opposition to the
project.

Our File No. I-91-366
Page 4


ANALYSIS

I.  Section 87407

    Section 87407 provides:

        No state administrative official, elected
    state officer, or designated employee of the
    Legislature shall make, participate in making, or
    use his or her official position to influence, any
    governmental decision directly relating to any
    person with whom he or she is negotiating, or has
    any arrangement concerning, prospective employment.

    You are a designated employee of the Coastal Commission.  As
such you are prohibited from making, participating in making, or
using your official position to influence, any governmental
decision directly relating to any person with whom you are
negotiating, or have any arrangement concerning, prospective
employment.

    What constitutes "making, participating in making, or using
official position to influence" is not defined in the new statute.
The Act does define "making, participating in making, and using
official position to influence" in the context of the conflict-of-
interest provisions of the Act.  Regulation 18700 and 18700.1
provide:

        A public official or designated employee
    "participates in the making of a governmental
    decision" when, acting within the authority of his
    or her position, he or she:

        (1)  Negotiates, without significant
    substantive review, with a governmental entity
    or private person regarding the decision; or

        (2)  Advises or makes recommendations to
    the decision-maker, either directly or without
    significant intervening substantive review,
    by:

            (A)  Conducting research or making
        any investigation which requires the
        exercise of judgment on the part of the
        official or designated employee and the
        purpose of which is to influence the
        decision; or

Our File No. I-91-366
Page 5


                    (B)  Preparing or presenting any
          report, analysis or opinion, orally or in
          writing, which requires the exercise of
          judgment on the part of the official or
          designated employee and the purpose of
          which is to influence the decision.

                    Regulation 18700(c).

          (a)  With regard to a governmental decision
     which is within or before an official's agency or
     an agency appointed by or subject to the budgetary
     control of his or her agency, the official is
     attempting to use his or her official position to
     influence the decision if, for the purpose of
     influencing the decision, the official contacts, or
     appears before, or otherwise attempts to influence,
     any member, officer, employee or consultant of the
     agency.  Attempts to influence include, but are not
     limited to, appearances or contacts by the official
     on behalf of a business entity, client, or
     customer.

                         * * *

          (c)  With regard to a governmental decision
     which is within or before an agency not covered by
     subsection (a), the official is attempting to use
     his or her official position to influence the
     decision if, for the purpose of influencing the
     decision, the official acts or purports to act on
     behalf of, or as the representative of, his or her
     agency to any member, officer, employee or
     consultant of an agency.  Such actions include, but
     are not limited to the use of official stationery.

                    Regulation 18700.1(a)
                    and (c).

          Until definitions are developed for Section 87407, the
     conflict-of-interest definition may be used as a guide.  Pursuant
     to this definition, a public official is using his or her official
     position to influence a decision where the official contacts, or
     appears before, or otherwise attempts to influence, any member,
     officer, employee or consultant of the official's agency or any
     agency appointed by or subject to the budgetary control of his or
     her agency.  (Regulation 18700.1.)  Moreover, an official is using
     his or her official position to influence a decision before any
     other agency if, for the purpose of influencing the decision, the
     official acts or purports to act on behalf of, or as the
     representative of, his or her agency.  Such actions include, but
     are not limited to the use of official stationery.  (Regulation
     18700.1(c).)

Our File No. I-91-366
Page 6


     Section 87407 does not define what constitutes "negotiating,"
or an "arrangement" concerning prospective employment.  However,
according to your facts, your question is premised on the need to
seek and arrange new employment in the face of possible layoffs at
the Coastal Commission.  Thus, we assume you will be
"negotiating," or making an "arrangement" concerning prospective
employment.

     However, Section 87407 also does not specify when a decision
is "directly related" to a person with whom an official is
negotiating, or has any arrangement concerning, prospective
employment.  Until the Commission has developed a regulatory
definition of "directly related to" we are advising conservatively
that governmental decisions directly relate to a potential
employer if the decision will financially affect the potential
employer, or if the employer has attempted to influence the agency
in any manner with respect to the decision.  The two persons you
have described would appear to be sufficiently interested in the
project to trigger the restrictions of Section 87407.

II.  Revolving Door Restrictions Under the Ethics Bill

     Effective January 1, 1991, the Legislature enacted a series
of statutes intended to establish ethical guidelines for public
officials.  The Act now provides that legislators, state
officeholders and designated employees of administrative agencies
are subject to new restrictions on their post-employment
activity.³  For designated employees of state agencies Section
37406 provides in relevant part:

          (d)(1)  No designated employee of a state
     administrative agency, any officer, employee, or
     consultant of a state administrative agency who
     holds a position which entails the making, or
     participation in the making, of decisions which may
     foreseeably have a material effect on any financial
     interest, and no member of a state administrative
     agency, for a period of one year after leaving
     office or employment, shall, for compensation, act
     as agent or attorney for, or otherwise represent,
     any other person, by making any formal or informal
     appearance, or by making any oral or written
     communication, before any state administrative
     agency, or officer or employee thereof, for which
     he or she worked or represented during the 12
     months before leaving office or employment, if the
     appearance or communication is made for the purpose
     of influencing administrative action, or
     influencing any action or proceeding involving the
     issuance, amendment, awarding, or revocation of a

_______________
³  These provisions do not apply to legislative staffers.

Our File No. I-91-366
Page 7


permit, license, grant, or contract, or the sale or
purchase of goods or property.  For purposes of
this paragraph, an appearance before a state
administrative agency does not include an
appearance in a court of law, before an
administrative law judge, or before a Worker's
Compensation Appeals Board.  The prohibition of
this paragraph shall only apply to designated
employees employed by a state administrative agency
on or after January 7, 1991.

* * *

     (e)  The prohibitions contained in
subdivisions (b), (c), and (d) shall not apply to
any individual subject to this section who is or
becomes an officer or employee of another state
agency, board, or commission if the appearance or
communication is for the purpose of influencing
legislative or administrative action on behalf of
the state agency, board, or commission.

     You are a designated employee of the Coastal Commission in a
position that your agency has determined entails the nature of
participating in making of decisions which may affect a financial
interest.  Pursuant to this section, you may not:

     1.  Act as representative, agent or attorney for any person
before the Coastal Commission or any other administrative agency
for which you have worked or represented during the 12 months
before leaving office or employment,

     2.  For compensation,

     3.  For the purpose of influencing administrative action or
any action or proceeding involving the issuance or amendment,
awarding, or revocation of a permit, license, grant, or contract,
or the sale or purchase of goods or property.  (Section 87406.)

     "Administrative action" is defined in Section 82002 as the
proposal, drafting, development, consideration, amendment,
enactment or defeat by any state agency of any rule, regulation or
other action in any rate-making proceeding or any quasi-
legislative proceeding.  The prohibition continues for one year
after you have ended your employment with the Coastal Commission.
(Section 87406.)  However, Section 87406(d)(1) exempts appearances
in a court of law; before an administrative law judge and,
appearances before the Worker's Compensation Appeals Board.

III.  Traditional Revolving Door

     However, even prior to the enactment of the new ethics
provisions of the Act, the Act did impose a lifetime restriction

Our File No. I-91-366
Page 8


on the activities of certain administrative officials under
certain circumstances. Sections 87401 and 87402 provide:

     No former state administrative official, after
the termination of his or her employment or term of
office, shall for compensation act as agent or
attorney for, or otherwise represent, any other
person (other than the State of California) before
any court or state administrative agency or any
officer or employee thereof by making any formal or
informal appearance, or by making any oral or
written communication with the intent to influence,
in connection with any judicial, quasi-judicial or
other proceeding if both of the following apply:

     (a) The State of California is a party or
has a direct and substantial interest.

     (b) The proceeding is one in which the
former state administrative official
participated.

               Section 87401.

     No former state administrative official, after
the termination of his or her employment or term of
office shall for compensation aid, advise, counsel,
consult or assist in representing any other person
(except the State of California) in any proceeding
in which the official would be prohibited from ap-
pearing under Section 87401.

               Section 87402.

     The California Coastal Commission is a "state administrative
agency." (Section 87400(a).) As stated above, as a member of the
Federal Consistency Staff of the California Coastal Commission,
the Coastal Commission has determined that you hold a position
that has decisionmaking authority. Thus, your position is one
with more than purely clerical, secretarial or ministerial
authority.⁴ Thus, according to Section 87400(b) you are a "state
administrative official."

     As a "state administrative official," Sections 87401 and
87402 prohibit you from:

     1. Representing another person;

     2. Before a court or administrative agency;

_________
⁴ Generally, activity is ministerial if it is a "administrative
act carried out in a prescribed manner not allowing for personal
discretion." (Webster's New World Dictionary.)

Our File No. I-91-366
Page 9


     3.  For compensation;

     4.  In a judicial, quasi-judicial or other proceeding;

     5.  Where the proceeding is one in which you participated;
and,

     6.  The state is a party or has a direct and substantial
interest.

     In addition, Section 87402 prohibits you from aiding,
advising, counseling, consulting or assisting any other person in
the proceeding.

     Section 87400 provides:

          (c)  "Judicial, quasi-judicial or other
proceeding" means any proceeding, application,
request for a ruling or other determination,
contract, claim, controversy, investigation,
charge, accusation, arrest or other particular
matter involving a specific party or parties in any
court or state administrative agency, including but
not limited to any proceeding governed by Chapter 5
(commencing with Section 11500) of Division 3 of
Title 2 of the Government Code.[5]

          (d)  "Participated" means to have taken part
personally and substantially through decision,
approval, disapproval, formal written
recommendation, rendering advice on a substantial
basis, investigation or use of confidential
information as an officer or employee, but
excluding approval, disapproval or rendering of
legal advisory opinions to an agency or agency
staff which do not involve a specific party or
parties.

     Clearly, general activities such as the production of a
newsletter are not judicial, quasi-judicial or other proceedings
under the Act.  (Fong Advice Letter, No. A-83-024.)  Thus, this
activity does not result in any revolving door problems.

     However, the monitoring of past federal consistency projects
and participating in the review of new negative determination and
environmental impact statements do appear to meet the definition
of a "judicial, quasi-judicial or other proceeding." (Section
87400(c).)  The submission of a negative determination statement

_______________
5    Government Code Sections 11500, et seq, sets forth state
agencies subject to the Administrative Procedures Act and the
procedures applicable to those agencies.

Our File No. I-91-366
Page 10


and draft EIS is a request for an official determination by the
Coastal Commission that the applicant's project will have only a
negligible effect on Coastal Commission jurisdiction. Thus, this
activity would also be a judicial, quasi-judicial or other
proceeding. (See, McGrath Advice Letter, No. A-90-085.)
Consequently, you will be barred from rendering any other
person before the Coastal Commission with respect to any federal
consistency review in which you monitored or participated.

However, your involvement with the EIR/EIS for the project
does not appear to have yet risen to the level of a "proceeding"
before the Coastal Commission, or apparently any other state
administrative agency. Currently, the matter continues to be a
quasi-judicial proceeding before the local agency. Section
87400(c) contemplates more substantive involvement, akin to
an application, request for a ruling or other determination,
contract, claim, controversy, investigation, or other particular
matter involving a specific party or parties in a court before
a state administrative agency. Thus, even if your participation
in rendering a written recommendation to the Coastal Commission by
way of your letters was personal participation, it
was not participation in a quasi-judicial proceeding before the
Coastal Commission or any other state agency.6

If you have any further questions regarding this matter,
please feel free to contact me at (916) 322-5901.7

Sincerely,

Scott Hallabrin
Acting General Counsel

By: John W. Wallace
     Counsel, Legal Division

SH:JWW:aa


6    The analysis would be different if the application was
submitted to the Coastal Commission to request an official
determination by the Coastal Commission on a project.

7    Copies of Commission regulations and Opinions are available in
many law libraries. Alternatively, copies of these materials and
Commission advice letters may be obtained from the Commission at a
cost of 10¢ per page.

acts or purports to act on behalf of, or as the
representative of, his or her agency. Such actions include, but
are not limited to the use of official stationery. 
(Regulation
1,8700.1(c) . )


Our page 6
Section 8740? does not define what constitutes 'rnegot iating, tr
or an rrarrangement" concerning prospective employment. However,
according to your facts, your question is prernised on the need to
seek and arrange nee, employment in the face of possible layoffs at
the coastal Commission. Thus, we assume you wiII be
"negotiating, " or making an I'arrangementrr concerning prospective
employment.
Hovrever, Section 87407 also does not specify when a decj-sion
is "directly related" to a person with whom an official 
is
negotiating, or has any arrangement concerning, prospective
employment. Until the Conmission has developed a regulatory
aefinition of "directly related to'r we are advising conservatively
that governmental decisions directly relate to a potential-
employer if the decision will- f inanci.ally affect the potential
employer, or if the employer has attempted to influence the agency
in any manner with respect to the decision. The two persons you
have described would appear to be sufficiently interested in the
project to trigger the restrictions of section a74o7.
II. 
Revolvino Door Restrictions Under the Ethics BiIl
Effective January l, 1991, the Legislature enacted a series
of statutes intended to establish ethical guj.deJ-ines for public
officials. 
The Act now provides that legislators, state
officeholders and designated employees of administrative agencies
are subiect to new restrictions on their post-emp Ioyr0ent
activity.3 
For designated employees of state agencies Section
87406 provides in relevant Part:
(d) (1) No designated employee of a state
administrative agency, any officer, employee, or
consul-tant of a state administrative agency who
holds a positlon which entails the making, or
participation in the making, of decisions which may
ioreseeably have a material effect on any financial
interest, and no member of a state administrative
agency, for a period of one year after leavj.ng
of f j.ce or employnent, shall, for compensation, act
as agent or attorney for, or otherwise represent,
any other person. by making any formal or informal
appearance, or by making any oral or written
communication, before any state administrative
agency, or officer or employee thereof, for which
he or she worked or represented during the 12
months before leaving office or employmen!, if the
appearance or communication is made for the purpose
of j,nf luenci.ng administrative action, or
influencing any action or proceeding involving the
issuance, amendment, awarding, or revocation of a
These provisions do not apply to legislative staffers.

-366
permit, Iicense, grant, or contract, or the sale or
purchase of goods or property. For purposes of
this paragraph, an appearance before a state
adninistrative agency does not include an
appearance in a court of law, before an
administrative 1a!, judge, or before the worker's
conpensation Appeals Board. The prohibition of
this paragraph shaII onJ.y appJ'y to designated
employees employed by a state administrative agency
on or after JanuarY 7, 1991.

embedding.first_500_words: November 26, 1991 Gerry Chalmers Federal Consistency Staff California Coastal Commission 45 Fremont, Suite 2000 San Francisco, CA 94105-2219 Re: Your Request for Informal Assistance Our File No. I-91-366 Dear Mr. Chalmers: This is in response to your letter requesting advice regarding your responsibilities as a member of the Federal Consistency Staff of the California Coastal Commission under the "revolving-door" and new "ethics" provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").1 Since your need to seek new employment at this time is uncertain and you have not become involved in negotiations for future employment, we are treating your letter as a request for informal assistance.2 QUESTIONS 1. As an intern with the California Coastal Commission, what restrictions are imposed on your participation in Coastal Commission matters relating to persons with whom you may be negotiating about, or may have arranged, prospective employment? 2. At the time you terminate your employment with the California Coastal Commission, what restrictions will there be on your post-employment activity? _______________ 1 Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory references are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated. Commission regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations Section 18000, et seq. All references to regulations are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations. 2 Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice. (Section 83114; Regulation 18329(c)(3).) 428 J Street, Suite 800 ● P.O. Box 807 ● Sacramento CA 95804-0807 ● (916) 322-5660 Our File No. I-91-366 Page 2 CONCLUSIONS 1. As a designated employee of the California Coastal Commission, you are prohibited from making, participating in making, or using your official position to influence, any governmental decision directly relating to any person with whom you are negotiating, or to have an arrangement concerning, prospective employment. 2. You may not represent or act as agent or attorney for any person before the California Coastal Commission for compensation, for the purpose of influencing administrative action for one year. In addition, you may not represent any other person before the California Coastal Commission with respect to any federal consistency review in which you monitored or participated. This restriction has no time limit. FACTS You have been an intern with the Coastal Commission's Federal Consistency Program since June, 1990. The Federal Consistency Program is based on the Federal Coastal Zone Management Act which gives the California Coastal Commission limited regulatory authority with respect to federal and federally permitted activities that affect the coastal zone. Under the Federal Coastal Zone Management Act, the Coastal Commission may review specified federal licenses and permits which affect land and water, or affect the resources of the coastal zone directly or indirectly through spill-over impacts. As an intern you are a designated employee required to complete an annual statement of economic interests. As an intern you have been involved in the following: 1. General activities such as the production of a newsletter to local governments describing the proceedings of workshops conducted by
